frameworks.name: Bayesian confidence
frameworks.one_line: confidence reports as posterior probability that the decision is correct

frameworks.name: non-Bayesian heuristic models
frameworks.one_line: simple rules using sensory uncertainty in non-normative ways

frameworks.name: perceptual categorization
frameworks.one_line: two-category decision tasks used to probe confidence behaviour

frameworks.name: signal-detection / sensory uncertainty access
frameworks.one_line: confidence circuits read low-level uncertainty representations

core_question: Do human confidence reports reflect Bayesian posterior probability of correctness, or are they better explained by non-Bayesian heuristics that still use sensory uncertainty?
key_assumptions: confidence is a reportable graded quantity
sensory uncertainty is internally represented and accessible
Bayesian confidence makes specific predictions linking uncertainty to reports
model comparison can adjudicate between Bayesian and heuristic accounts
manipulations: model comparison metric and Bayesian-model flexibility
key_findings: subjects incorporate sensory uncertainty into confidence reports
behaviour is not fully consistent with the Bayesian confidence hypothesis
simple heuristic models using uncertainty fit confidence better than Bayesian models
conclusions hold across tasks, uncertainty manipulations, and modelling choices
interpretations: confidence-reporting brain areas can access low-level sensory-uncertainty representations
rational accounts of confidence likely require implementational constraints
